coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot-based afterglow composite with tunable room temperature phosphorescence and thermally activated delayed fluorescence and their anti-counterfeiting and encryption application
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Jing
Lei, Mingxiu
Yan, Lingpeng
Chen, Lin
Yang, Yongzhen
Zheng, Jingxia
Liu, Xuguang
Xu, Bingshe
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) based afterglow materials have attracted much attention owing to their low toxicity, environmental protection, stable luminescence, abundant raw materials, and low cost. However, CD-based afterglow materials with both room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) characteristics are rarely reported, which greatly limits their in-depth application in advanced anti-counterfeiting, intelligent sensing, and other fields. In this paper, Cu, N co-doped CDs (Cu, N-CDs) with dual emission centers were synthesized via a simple solvothermal method by heteroatom doping to enhance the spin–orbit coupling of CDs and regulate the luminescence properties of CDs. Then, boric acid (BA) was used as the matrix to synthesize Cu, N-CDs@BA composites by matrix-assisted method, and the electron transfer enhancement afterglow was realized by the inorganic electronic defects of the matrix. Cu, N-CDs@BA can realize the mutual conversion of TADF and RTP by changing the excitation wavelength or increasing the temperature. At the excitation of 285 nm, the room temperature afterglow of Cu, N-CDs@BA exhibit TADF, and the average afterglow lifetime is 94.21 ms. At the excitation of 365 nm, they exhibit RTP, and the average afterglow lifetime is 347.96 ms. Based on three different emission properties (fluorescence, RTP, and TADF), Cu, N-CDs@BA was applied in anti-counterfeiting and encryption, which shows good application potential.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) based afterglow materials have attracted much attention owing to their low toxicity, environmental protection, stable luminescence, abundant raw materials, and low cost. However, CD-based afterglow materials with both room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) characteristics are rarely reported, which greatly limits their in-depth application in advanced anti-counterfeiting, intelligent sensing, and other fields. In this paper, Cu, N co-doped CDs (Cu, N-CDs) with dual emission centers were synthesized via a simple solvothermal method by heteroatom doping to enhance the spin–orbit coupling of CDs and regulate the luminescence properties of CDs. Then, boric acid (BA) was used as the matrix to synthesize Cu, N-CDs@BA composites by matrix-assisted method, and the electron transfer enhancement afterglow was realized by the inorganic electronic defects of the matrix. Cu, N-CDs@BA can realize the mutual conversion of TADF and RTP by changing the excitation wavelength or increasing the temperature. At the excitation of 285 nm, the room temperature afterglow of Cu, N-CDs@BA exhibit TADF, and the average afterglow lifetime is 94.21 ms. At the excitation of 365 nm, they exhibit RTP, and the average afterglow lifetime is 347.96 ms. Based on three different emission properties (fluorescence, RTP, and TADF), Cu, N-CDs@BA was applied in anti-counterfeiting and encryption, which shows good application potential.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new kind of zero dimensional nanomaterials, not only inherit the advantages of good biocompatibility and abundant raw material sources from traditional carbon materials, but also own unique optical properties [1–3], which endows them potential applications in optoelectronic devices [4–7], biological imaging [8,9], solar cell [10,11], anti-counterfeiting and encryption [12–14]. Since discovered in 2004 [15], the synthesis and photoluminescence of CDs have attracted wide attention. Photoluminescence CDs have been categorized into fluorescence (FL) and afterglow [16–18]. It's well known that afterglow of CDs, including room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF), have irreplaceable advantages in biological, optoelectronic, and energy-related fields with long-lived excitons from the dimension of time, which exhibit more advantages than fluorescent materials [19,20].

CD-based afterglow materials have the merits of easy preparation, wavelength tunability, and no doping of rare earth elements, and their lifetime can be improved by enhancing spin–orbit coupling (SOC) and suppressing non-radiative quenching of the triplet state [21], which will open up a new way for the development of afterglow materials with environmental friendliness, stable luminescence, and inexpensive cost. In 2012, Lin et al. firstly prepared the afterglow CDs with stable FL and RTP by doping Pb2+ [22], and Tetsuka et al. obtained the amino-functionalized graphite quantum dots by hydrothermal treatment and annealing of graphene oxide sheets under alkaline conditions, which showed sub-second delayed fluorescence properties [23]. The research on RTP and TADF CDs have been continuously reported, and the afterglow properties and mechanisms of CDs have gradually attracted the attention of researchers [24–26].

To date, the realization of afterglow properties undergoes intersystem crossing (ISC) and reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) processes, which can be effectively promoted by heteroatom doping as well as matrix assistance. Co-doping of non-metal atoms (B and N, N and P, etc.) can increase the content of functional groups (e.g., C = O and C = N) of CDs promoting ISC and RISC processes, thereby promoting ISC and RISC [27]. After doping metal atoms with more electrons and unoccupied orbitals as well as larger atomic radius into CDs, the electron density distribution and energy gaps of CDs are changed drastically, which enhances the SOC ability and effectively facilitates the ISC process [28]. Among them, Cu atom with a higher electron transfer probability can effectively enhance SOC and further promote ISC [29]. Currently, it has been reported that Cu, N-CDs applied in photocatalysis were synthesized by Cu, N co-doping to promote the electron transfer of CDs [30]. However, the Cu, N-CDs with dual emission centers for advanced information encryption and anti-counterfeiting have not been reported. Besides, the bonding between CDs and matrix can effectively reduce energy difference (ΔE ST) of system, thus promoting the ISC between excited triplet and singlet states. The matrix-assisted method embeds CDs into the matrix through covalent or hydrogen bonds, which can fix CDs to reduce their non-radiative decay while suppress the quenching of the excited triplet states by external air and water [31-33]. The self-protective method protects the excited triplet state by constructing a polymer chain on the surface of the CDs while limits the vibrational and rotational energy dissipation of the CDs luminescence center [34,35]. However, owing to the limitations of the synergistic effect of stabilized excited triplet states and ΔE ST between the excited singlet and triplet states, most of the CD-based afterglow materials exhibit only RTP property, limiting their applications in advanced information encryption as well as multistage bioimaging. Based on the sensitivity of RTP and TADF luminescence intensity to temperature, CD-based afterglow materials with RTP and TADF dual-mode afterglow emission can construct ratio-type intelligent sensors, which can effectively avoid the adverse effects of self-FL interference and human observation error [36,37]. At the same time, the FL, RTP, and TADF multiple emission properties of CD-based afterglow materials with tunable RTP and TADF can improve anti-counterfeiting and encryption levels, reduce forgery rates, and thus improve security [38,39]. Therefore, the exploration of CD-based afterglow materials with RTP and TADF dual-mode emission will further promote their in-depth application in the fields of advanced sensing, anti-counterfeiting and encryption.

Materials: All chemicals are of analytical reagent grade and were used directly upon receipt without further purification. Folic acid (FA, 97 %) was purchased from Maya Reagent. Copper chloride dihydrate (CuCl2•2H2O), citric acid, and N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (KH-792) were obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. Ethanol (99.5 %) was purchased from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Boric acid (BA) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Deionized water was obtained from the Milli-Q system.

Characterization: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the Cu, N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs@BA were recorded on a JEOL JEM 2100, which used ultra-thin carbon films as support substrate. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were carried out by using Rigaku Smart Lab X-ray diffractometer (Cu target, λ = 1.5418 Å). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a Tensor 27 spectrometer (Bruker). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterizations were conducted on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (America). Fluorescence and afterglow spectra were measured using a F-7100 spectrometer (Hitachi). The temperature-dependent afterglow spectra were obtained using a FLS-980 spectrometer (Edinburgh). The oxygen vacancy defect characterizations were recorded by Bruker A300-10/12 electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrometer.

Synthesis of Cu, N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs@BA: FA (50 mg) and CuCl2•2H2O (190 mg) were dissolved in 15 mL of ethanol. The solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL), heated at 200 °C for 6 h, then the crude product was obtained after cooling down to room temperature naturally. The crude product was filtered through a nylon filtration membrane filter (0.22 μm) and removed ethanol by rotary evaporation. The crude products of Cu, N-CDs were further dialyzed against DI water with a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 24 h to remove unreacted reagents and small molecular species. The solid products of Cu, N-CDs were obtained after freeze-drying for further use. 40 mL of BA solution (50 mg/mL) was mixed with 2 mL of Cu, N-CDs solution (0.2 mg/mL) in a backer and the mixed solution was ultrasonic for 30 min. Then, the backer was calcinated at 180 °C for 5 h and cooled down to room temperature naturally. The final Cu, N-CDs@BA powder was obtained by grinding.

Synthesis of BA-X at different temperatures or for different times: To evaluate the afterglow performance of BA and to screen the optimal matrix ligands, BA-X was obtained by calcining BA at different temperatures or for different times:

40 mL of BA solution (50 mg/mL) was ultrasonic for 30 min in a backer and then the backer was put in an oven at 120, 140, 160, 180, and 200 °C for 5 h, respectively, and cooled down to room temperature naturally. The final BA-120, BA-140, BA-160, BA-180, and BA-200 powder were obtained by grinding.

40 mL of BA solution (50 mg/mL) was ultrasonic for 30 min in a backer and then the backer was put in an oven at 180 °C for 3, 4, and 5 h, respectively, and cooled down to room temperature naturally. The final BA-3, BA-4, and BA-5 powder were obtained by grinding.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of Cu, N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs@BA
ce:section.ce:para: The Cu, N-CDs@BA synthesized by heating the mixed solution of Cu, N-CDs and BA at 180 °C for 5 h has strong solid-state afterglow properties at room temperature. Their afterglow colors at short-wavelength and long-wavelength excitation are different (Fig. 1 a). At the excitation of 285 nm, the composite exhibits a blue afterglow visible to the naked eye for up to 8 s after turning off the UV lamp, while at the excitation of 365 nm, the composite emits green afterglow after turning off the UV lamp. First, the microstructure of Cu, N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs@BA was observed. TEM and HRTEM images show that Cu, N-CDs are spherical particles with the average particle size of 4.23 nm and crystalline interior structure (Fig. 1b). The average particle size of the dispersed quasi-spherical particles in Fig. 1c is about 4.23 nm, and obvious lattice fringes with 0.21 nm of crystal plane spacing can be observed in the HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1c), which are consistent with Cu, N-CDs, indicating that Cu, N-CDs have been successfully dispersed in the composite material, and their particle size and structure have not changed significantly. The phase structure and chemical compositions of Cu, N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs@BA are further characterized (Fig. S1, 1d − i). In the XRD pattern of Cu, N-CDs@BA, B2O3 diffraction peaks are appeared at 2θ = 14.70°, 27.75°, and 41.58°, except for the diffraction peak from the graphite (002) crystal plane at 2θ = 23.00°, indicating the presence of B2O3 structure in the composite [33,40] (Fig. 1d). The FT-IR and XPS characterization results shows that Cu, N-CDs have abundant nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface (Fig. 1e, S1c − g, respectively). Among them, the C = O and C = N are beneficial to enhance SOC, promote ISC and RISC, and thus facilitate afterglow emission [41,42]. In the FT-IR spectra of Cu, N-CDs@BA, the characteristic peaks at 1400 − 1500, 1282, and 1197 cm−1 are attributable to the stretching vibration of B − O, B − C, and the asymmetric stretching vibration of B − O in the B − O − B bond connecting the triangular and tetrahedral B atomic units, respectively. Moreover, the adsorption peaks at 725 and 655 cm−1 are derived from the bending vibration of B − O − H [43,44] (Fig. 1e). These results indicates that B2O3 matrix may be formed by the gradual dehydration of BA during the heating process, and Cu, N-CDs are embedded into the matrix through B − C covalent bonds. The XPS results of Cu, N-CDs@BA are shown in Fig. 1f − i. The high-resolution B1s shows that three peaks at 194.082, 193.379, and 192.469 eV, which are attributed to B − O, B2O3, and BCO2 [45,46], respectively. The results further indicate the formation of covalent bonds between B2O3 matrix and B − C, which is consistent with the results of XRD and FT-IR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of Cu, N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs@BA
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of Cu, N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs@BA were further systematically studied. The FL spectrum of Cu, N-CDs aqueous solution shows dual-emission properties (Fig. S2b). The absorption bands at 315 and 375 nm in the UV–vis spectrum are derived from the π → π* transition of the C = C bond and the n → π* transition of C = O/C = N, respectively [47]. The optimal emission peak is located at 420 nm and the optimal excitation wavelength is 370 nm (Fig. S2c). At the excitation of 375 nm, the average fluorescence lifetimes of the emission peaks at 420 and 465 nm are 5.53 and 7.79 ns, respectively, indicating that the two FL emission peaks are originated from different luminescent centers (Fig. S2d) (Fitting parameters listed in Table S3). Cu, N-CDs show no fluorescence in solid state owing to aggregation-induced quenching effect, while Cu, N-CDs@BA exhibits good solid-state FL and afterglow properties because of the formation of B2O3 matrix (Fig. 2, S3). Compared with the absorption spectrum of Cu, N-CDs dispersed in aqueous solution, the absorption spectrum of Cu, N-CDs@BA shows the red shift owing to the aggregation of Cu, N-CDs in BA matrix (Fig. 2a) [48]. In Fig. 2b, Cu, N-CDs@BA shows excitation-dependent afterglow, and its afterglow emission peaks gradually redshift with increasing excitation wavelength. To further analyze FL and afterglow properties of Cu, N-CDs@BA, the FL and afterglow emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths were tested. At 285 and 305 nm excitation, the FL emission peaks are located at 433 and 435 nm, respectively, and their corresponding afterglow emission peaks are located at 434 and 435 nm, respectively (Fig. 2c, S3a − b). The FL and afterglow emission peaks overlap, which belongs to the feature of DF. At the excitation of 325 nm, the afterglow emission peak has a slight red shift compared with the FL emission peak, but there is still a large overlap region (Fig. S3c). When the excitation wavelengths are 345 and 365 nm, the afterglow emission peaks have a significant red shift compared with the FL, the afterglow mainly shows RTP property (Fig. S3d, 2d). The afterglow decay curves of composite monitored at 434 and 500 nm are given in Fig. 2 e, and the fitting parameters are shown in Table S4. In both cases, the decay curves are fitted by a tri-exponential function. The average lifetimes of DF (Ex. = 285 nm, Em. = 434 nm) and RTP (Ex. = 365 nm, Em. = 500 nm) are 94.21 and 347.96 ms, respectively. Moreover, the afterglow decay components at 285 and 365 nm excitation are different, suggesting that the decay modes of DF and RTP are different under different external excitation conditions. The DF properties of Cu, N-CDs@BA were further explored, and the temperature-responsive afterglow behavior (77 − 450 K) at 285 and 365 nm excitation was analyzed (Fig. 2f − i). At the excitation of 285 nm, the afterglow intensity of Cu, N-CDs@BA continually decreases with increasing temperature in the range of 77 − 345 K, while the afterglow intensity increases with the increasing temperature from 345 to 405 K. The gradually quenching of afterglow intensity with the further increased temperature from 405 to 450 K is due to the enhancement of non-radiative decay. Meanwhile, as the temperature increases, the temperature-dependent afterglow decay curve of Cu, N-CDs@BA at 285 nm excitation shows a decrease trend with the longest lifetime of 140 ms. Therefore, the DF of Cu, N-CDs@BA at the excitation of 285 nm belongs to TADF. At the excitation of 365 nm, the phosphorescence intensity weakens with the increasing of temperature from 77 to 345 K. When the temperature increases from 345 to 420 K, the afterglow intensity enhances continuously with the blue shift of afterglow emission center, indicating that the afterglow type is converted from RTP to TADF. Finally, when the temperature continues to rise, the non-radiative attenuation caused by heat dissipation gradually increases and the afterglow gradually quenches. At the excitation of 365 nm, the temperature-dependent afterglow decay curve of Cu, N-CDs@BA shows the decreasing trend with the increasing temperature, and the longest lifetime of 420 ms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Afterglow sources and tuning mechanism of Cu, N-CDs@BA
ce:section.ce:para: There are currently two explanations for the source of afterglow, in which organic material is mostly involved in the stability of its excited triplet state, while inorganic material is derived from the capture and release of electrons by its internal electronic defects [49,50]. According to the structure characterization results, the Cu, N-CDs@BA is a kind of inorganic–organic hybrid composites, whose afterglow source may be related to its organic and inorganic emission centers. The n → π* from C = O and C = N of Cu, N-CDs can efficiently promote the ISC, and the formation of glassy state B2O3 matrix can protect the triplet state of Cu, N-CDs from being quenched by the external moisture and oxygen, thus facilitating the triplet state to capture the excitons of Cu, N-CDs [51]. The empty p-orbits from B atoms of the Cu, N-CDs@BA can form p-π conjugated system by withdrawing the π electrons, thus efficiently lowering the LUMO level of system [44,45]. Meanwhile, the formation of B − C covalent bond reduces the ΔE ST, and then realize the ISC between the excited triplet state and singlet state [52].

Moreover, the glassy B2O3 matrix of Cu, N-CDs@BA composites may be one of afterglow sources. It has been reported that the product from pure BA powder after high-temperature calcination (250 °C) show ultralong room-temperature afterglow, and their afterglow originates from inorganic electronic defects of B2O3 [53]. In this work, the effects of calcining conditions on the structure and afterglow properties of the products from BA were investigated in terms of both heating temperature and heating time. When the heating time is fixed at 5 h and the reaction temperature is increased (from 120 to 200 °C), it is found that the products synthesized at 120–160 ℃ all show powdery appearance (Fig. S4a), and the BA-180 possess the glassy appearance (Fig. S4d). The XRD and FT-IR results of the products from BA solution at different heating temperatures show that the heating temperature is the main influence factor on the glassy structure. With the increase of temperature, the diffraction peak corresponding to HBO2-III at 2θ = 28.35° gradually enhances, while the diffraction peak of H3BO3 at 2θ = 27.80° gradually weakens [54]. When the heating temperature reaches to 160 °C, the characteristic peaks from HBO2-II and HBO2-I begin to appear at 2θ = 21.38° and 2θ = 22.00°, respectively [55]. When the heating temperature is 180 °C, it can be seen that the sharp fingerprint peaks change to wide diffraction peaks with the amorphous structure from the XRD spectrum of BA-180, which corresponds to B2O3, indicating the formation of B2O3 (Fig. 3 a). The FT-IR spectra of BA-120, BA-140, and BA-160 show that absorption bands between 1420–1320 cm−1 range are assigned to the stretching vibration of B–O, and the absorption peaks located at 635 and 750 cm−1 are attributed to the bending vibration of B–O–H [44]. The characteristic peaks in the range of 600–700 cm−1 decrease with increased temperature. When the temperature reaches to 180 °C, the characteristic peak at 1220 cm−1 appears, which belongs to the asymmetric stretching vibration of O–B–O between connecting the triangular and tetrahedral B atom units [44], indicating that the formation of amorphous B2O3 with the increase of dehydration degree of BA. It is consistent with the XRD results (Fig. 3b). Moreover, the afterglow properties were characterized, and the results showed that BA-120 and BA-140 have no afterglow emission, and BA-160 just shows weak afterglow emission at 285 and 365 nm excitation (Fig. S4b). The afterglow property of BA-180 is similar to those of the Cu, N-CDs@BA composite, but the afterglow lifetime and intensity at naked eyes are all weaker than those of Cu, N-CDs@BA (Fig. S4c–e). With the temperature further increase, the afterglow of BA-200 significantly weakens. When the heating temperature is fixed at 180 °C and the reaction time was changed from 3, 4, to 5 h. Although BA-3, BA-4, and BA-5 all appear glassy, there are no obvious structural changes with prolonged heating time according to the XRD patterns and FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3c). With the heating time prolonging, the amorphous diffraction peak gradually increases, the O − H stretching vibration of water at 2800 − 3500 cm−1 and B − O − H bending vibration at 600 − 750 cm−1 decrease, and the O − B − O stretching vibration gradually enhances, suggesting that the relative content of B2O3 increases with the continuous dehydration reaction induced by the prolonged heating time (Fig. 3d). The afterglow lifetimes show that the afterglows of BA-3 and BA-4 are weaker than that of BA-5. It can be seen that glassy B2O3 with afterglow property can be obtained by calcining BA at 180 °C for 5 h. Further, the afterglow property of Cu, N-CDs@BA synthesized under this condition are better than that of BA-180, confirming that glassy B2O3 is served as another afterglow emission origination of Cu, N-CDs@BA.

According to the above structural analysis, the formation of glassy B2O3 is crucial to the enhanced afterglow of Cu, N-CDs@BA compared with BA-180. To further resolve the effect of B2O3 matrix on the enhanced afterglow, the afterglow lifetime and photoluminescence quantum yield of BA-180 (Cu, N-CDs@BA without Cu, N-CDs) were characterized. Fig. 3e and 3f are the afterglow decay curves of BA-180 at 285 and 365 nm excitation, respectively. Different from the tri-exponential decay of Cu, N-CDs@BA, the decay curves of BA-180 are fitted to dual-exponential decay. The afterglow lifetime of BA-180 at 285 and 365 nm excitation is 71.729 and 111.507 ms, respectively (Fig. 3e − f) (Fitting parameters listed in Table S5), which is significantly shorter than that of Cu, N-CDs@BA (Fitting parameters listed in Table S4). This result suggests that there may be an electron transfer process between Cu, N-CDs and B2O3 matrix after embedding Cu, N-CDs into B2O3 matrix, which thus prolongs the afterglow lifetime and enhances the afterglow emission [46]. The quantum yield of Cu, N-CDs@BA is 18.3 % and 13.8 % at 285 and 365 nm excitation, respectively. And the quantum yield of BA-180 is 5.7 % and 6.8 % at 285 and 365 nm excitation, respectively. Obviously, Cu, N-CDs@BA possesses higher quantum yield, which may be due to the electron transfer process between Cu, N-CDs and B2O3 matrix. Glassy B2O3 matrix can effectively inhibit the aggregation-induced quenching of Cu, N-CDs, and at the same time, it may be captured excitons of Cu, N-CDs through its internal electron defects under UV irradiation, thereby achieving enhanced afterglow. In the inorganic afterglow system, the EPR spectra is always used to verify the existence of electron defects. In order to further figure out the electron transfer process between glassy B2O3 matrix and Cu, N-CDs in Cu, N-CDs@BA, Cu, N-CDs, BA-180, and Cu, N-CDs@BA were characterized by EPR spectra, and the effect of glassy B2O3 matrix on the afterglow properties of Cu, N-CDs@BA was investigated. The sharp EPR signal of Cu, N-CDs can be observed in Fig. 3g, indicating that there are unpaired electrons in Cu, N-CDs. Fig. 3h is the EPR spectra of Cu, N-CDs@BA and BA-180 under dark condition and UV irradiation at 285 and 365 nm. Under dark condition, EPR signal can be observed in BA-180, and the g factor is 2.003, which generally belongs to the electrons trapped in oxygen vacancies (2.000 − 2.003) [56]. It indicates that the inorganic B2O3 matrix contains certain oxygen vacancies. After embedding the Cu, N-CDs into amorphous B2O3 matrix, EPR signal can still be detected in dark condition, which is significantly weaker than that of Cu, N-CDs and slightly stronger than that of BA-180, indicating that amorphous glassy B2O3 matrix can effectively capture the unpaired electrons in Cu, N-CDs by paramagnetic defects [57]. Under UV irradiation, BA-180 can generate a large number of electrons and holes, part of the electrons is captured by the oxygen vacancy defects and then thermally released again at room temperature, thus generating afterglow. Consistent with the dark condition, the EPR signals of Cu, N-CDs@BA at 285 and 365 nm excitation are stronger than those of BA-180, indicating that the triplet excitons generated from Cu, N-CDs can be effectively captured into the electron defects of inorganic B2O3 matrix at the external excitation, which further promotes enhanced afterglow.

The conversion between RTP and TADF is generally related to ΔE ST. The FL and afterglow spectra of Cu, N-CDs@BA at 77 K were further characterized. The changes of ΔE ST of Cu, N-CDs@BA at different excitation wavelengths were investigated to reveal the afterglow tuning mechanism. Consistent with FL and afterglow spectra at the room temperature, the afterglow emission of Cu, N-CDs@BA at 77 K gradually redshifts compared with its FL emission with the increase of excited wavelengths (Fig. 4 a). The values of the excited singlet state (S 1), triplet state (T 1) at different excited wavelengths is calculated by the E = 1240/λ according to the FL and afterglow emission peaks in Fig. 4b − f, and ΔE ST is calculated by ΔE ST = S 1–T 1 (Table 1 ) [58]. The ΔE ST of Cu, N-CDs@BA at 285 nm excitation is 0.034 eV. With the excited wavelengths increase, the ΔE ST gradually increases. When the excited wavelength is 365 nm, the ΔE ST is 0.491 eV. According to the reported literatures [59,60], the smaller ΔE ST (<0.3 eV) favors the rapid activation of RISC at the excitation of thermal activation energy, which thus promotes the production of TADF, whereas materials with the larger ΔE ST (>0.3 eV) mainly exhibit RTP emission. Therefore, the Cu, N-CDs@BA exhibits tunable excitation-dependent afterglow properties at room temperature. Among them, the afterglow obtained at 285 and 305 nm excitation exhibits TADF (ΔE ST < 0.3 eV), while the afterglow obtained at 325, 345, and 365 nm excitation exhibits RTP (ΔE ST > 0.3 eV).

With the above discussion of the structure and optical properties of Cu, N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs@BA, the afterglow enhancement and afterglow tuning mechanism of Cu N-CDs@BA in this work can be proposed. The surface of Cu, N-CDs is found to be enrich in functional groups such as − NH, −OH, and − COOH by the previous structural analysis (Fig. S1), and in addition to the presence of B2O3 matrix, B − C, B − O, and other covalent bonds are formed in the Cu, N-CDs@BA (Fig. 1d − i). Therefore, the B2O3 matrix can immobilize Cu, N-CDs through spatial confinement effect and inter/intramolecular reactions: covalent bonds such as B − C and B − O, and hydrogen bonding interactions with functional groups such as − NH2, −OH, and − COOH on the surface of the Cu, N-CDs (Fig. 5 a). The previous discussion of the interaction mode between the B2O3 matrix and the Cu, N-CDs reveals that there is an electron transfer process between the electrons of Cu, N-CDs and the electronic defects of the B2O3 matrix (Fig. 5). After absorbing the UV excitation energy, the excited electrons of Cu, N-CDs@BA transition to the excited state, and the inorganic electronic defects (S trap) of B2O3 matrix located between S 1 and T 1 are able to capture and re-release some of the triplet excitons to realize the afterglow enhancement, and there are two detrapping forms of trapped excitons: they revert back to excited singlet state to realize the TADF under the action of the thermal activation energy, or they transition to the excited triplet state for the realization of the low temperature phosphorescence emission (LTP) (Fig. 5b).

Combining the optical properties of Cu, N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs@BA with the analysis of the variation of ΔE ST in the Cu, N-CDs@BA system, it can be concluded that the dual-emission nature of Cu, N-CDs and the electron transfer process between Cu, N-CDs and B2O3 matrix endow the Cu, N-CDs@BA with the unique tunable RTP and TADF afterglow properties. The generation of FL, RTP, and TADF of Cu, N-CDs@BA is demonstrated in Fig. 5c. The specific process is as follows: at 285 nm excitation, the Cu, N-CDs are dominated by FL emission at 465 nm. Therefore, at 285 nm excitation, the FL emission of Cu, N-CDs@BA is dominated by the S 1 state (433 nm), its triplet excitons can be captured by the electron-deficient state S trap located between S 1 and T 1 (process ①), and owing to the small energy difference between S trap and S 1, the excitons can return to the S 1 state at room temperature (process ②) and then radiatively transition to the ground state to realize TADF (S 0 → S 1 → S trap → S 1 → S 0); while at low temperature, the electron captured by the S trap state transition to the excited triplet state T 1 (process ③), and then radiatively transition to the ground state to produce low LTP (S 0 → S 1 → S trap → T 1 → S 0). At 365 nm UV excitation, the FL emission of Cu, N-CDs is dominated by 420 nm. Therefore, at 365 nm excitation, the FL emission of the Cu, N-CDs@BA is dominated by S 1* (413 nm), and owing to the large energy difference between the S trap and S 1* state, the exciton preferentially transition to the T 1* state (process ②) after the exciton delocalization and then return to the ground state in the form of the radiative transition and generate RTP, and the transition process is S 0 → S 1*→S trap → T 1*→S 0. With the gradual increasing of temperature, the exciton in the T 1* state return to the S 1* state after obtaining thermal activation energy (process ③) and thus generate TADF, whose process is S 0 → S 1*→S trap → T 1 → S 1*→S 0.

The excellent TADF and RTP inter-tuned afterglow properties of Cu, N-CDs@BA are expected to have great potential in the field of anti-counterfeiting and encryption. In order to increase the level of anti-counterfeiting and encryption, a triple anti-counterfeiting and encryption system of FL, RTP, and TADF was constructed, and blue CDs (B-CDs) with only FL properties were fabricated in accordance with previous work [48], which synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid and silane coupling agent KH792 as the precursor. Cu, N-CDs@BA and B-CDs were used for multiple anticounterfeiting graphics and information encryption (Fig. 6 ).

The Cu, N-CDs@BA were used for the line drawing of the school emblem, the B-CDs were used for the bottom color filling part of the school emblem, and the triple anti-counterfeiting applications of FL, TADF, and RTP were realized by switching the light source on and off and changing the excitation wavelengths (Fig. 6a). When irradiated together by 285 and 365 nm UV lamps, the anti-counterfeiting icons are in FL mode, and the blue “TYUT” and “1902” logos, the outline of the blue school emblem, and the blue background color can be clearly observed; when the 285 nm UV lamp is turned off, the TADF anti-counterfeiting mode is activated, the blue bottom color is disappeared, and only the blue “TYUT” and “1902” logos and the outline of the school emblem are observed; when the 365 nm UV lamp is turned off, the anti-counterfeiting mode is changed to the RTP mode, and the blue logos and outlines are changed to green.

The Cu, N-CDs@BA were used for writing the correct message, and the B-CDs were used for interfering information to realize the information encryption (Fig. 6b). At 285 and 365 nm UV excitation, the error information “8689” encoded together by the Cu, N-CDs@BA and B-CDs displays, and when the 285 nm UV light is turned off, the blue correct message “0504” solely encoded by the Cu, N-CDs@BA shows, while after the 365 nm UV light source turned off, the correct message “0504” appears in green color.

The above results indicate that the FL, RTP, and TADF triple emission of Cu, N-CDs@BA is conducive to the realization of multilevel anti-counterfeiting and encryption, which further improve the information security. Moreover, Cu, N-CDs@BA has the potential to be used as the smart optical anti-counterfeiting and encryption material for the construction of advanced optical anti-counterfeiting and encryption systems.

In summary, Cu, N-CDs with dual emission centers and afterglow emission potential were synthesized by the Cu, N co-doping method using FA and CuCl2 as precursors. As BA can form glassy B2O3 by calcination, it was used as the matrix material to prepare Cu, N-CDs@BA with room-temperature afterglow by the matrix-assisted method. The afterglow enhancement of Cu, N-CDs@BA originates from the electron transfer between Cu, N-CDs and BA matrix with oxygen vacancy defects. The unpaired electrons in Cu, N-CDs can be captured by oxygen vacancy defects in glassy B2O3, and the electron capture and re-release can enhance the afterglow of composites. The afterglow tuning mechanism of excitation-dependent RTP and TADF originates from the dual-emission of Cu, N-CDs and the energy level matching between Cu, N-CDs and BA matrix. With increasing temperature, Cu, N-CDs@BA exhibits TADF and RTP properties at 285 and 365 nm excitation, respectively. Cu, N-CDs@BA with FL, RTP, and TADF three different luminescence modes can effectively construct multiple anti-counterfeiting icons and information encryption processes, which provides a potential and feasible idea for the construction of advanced optical anti-counterfeiting and information encryption system.
